title: Shabu Shabu Thinly Sliced Beef Hot Pot

seo.title: Shabu Shabu Recipe: Authentic Japanese Beef Hot Pot
seo.metaDescription: Master the art of Shabu Shabu with our authentic Japanese beef hot pot recipe. Learn how to make this delicious, interactive dinner at home with thinly sliced beef and fresh vegetables.
longTailKeywords: easy Shabu Shabu beef hot pot
thinly sliced beef hot pot recipe
lowCompetitionKeywords: what to serve with Shabu Shabu
seo.h1: Authentic Shabu Shabu Recipe: Japanese Beef Hot Pot
h2Headings: Ingredients You'll Need for Shabu Shabu
How to Make Shabu Shabu (Step-by-Step Guide)
Tips for the Best Shabu Shabu Experience
seo.imageAlt: A steaming pot of authentic Japanese Shabu Shabu with thinly sliced beef, vegetables, and dipping sauces on a wooden table.
seo.imageCaption: Gather around for a delicious and interactive meal with this traditional Japanese Shabu Shabu beef hot pot recipe.

description: Shabu Shabu is a delightful Japanese hot pot dish featuring thinly sliced beef and fresh vegetables cooked quickly in a flavorful dashi broth. It is an interactive and communal dining experience, perfect for sharing with family and friends. Enjoy dipping the tender meat and crisp vegetables into rich ponzu or sesame sauce for an unforgettable meal.

ingredients.item: Very thinly sliced beef ribeye or sirloin

ingredients.notes: thinly sliced or cut into decorative shapes
grated for Goma Dare dipping sauce

instructions.title: Prepare the Dashi Broth
instructions.text: Gently wipe the kombu with a damp cloth. In a large hot pot or donabe, add 8 cups of water and the kombu. Let it soak for 30 minutes if time allows, then heat slowly over medium-low heat. Just before the water boils and small bubbles appear around the kombu, remove the kombu. Do not let it boil vigorously to prevent bitterness.

instructions.title: Prepare Ingredients
instructions.text: While the dashi is infusing, arrange all your vegetables and tofu on a large platter. Slice the napa cabbage, shiitake mushrooms, enoki mushrooms, carrots, and tofu into easy-to-handle, bite-sized pieces. Arrange the thinly sliced beef neatly on a separate platter. Prepare your dipping sauces in small individual bowls.

instructions.title: Make Dipping Sauces
instructions.text: For Ponzu Sauce: Combine 1/2 cup soy sauce, 1/4 cup rice vinegar, 2 tbsp lemon juice, and 2 tbsp dashi or water. Mix well. For Goma Dare Sesame Sauce: In a separate bowl, whisk together 1/4 cup toasted sesame paste, 2 tbsp soy sauce, 1 tbsp rice vinegar, 1 tsp sugar, 2 tbsp dashi or water, and 1 grated small clove of garlic until smooth.

instructions.title: Set Up the Hot Pot
instructions.text: Place your hot pot or donabe with the dashi broth on a portable burner in the center of your dining table. Bring the broth to a gentle simmer. Provide each diner with a small bowl for their cooked items and a small bowl for each dipping sauce.

instructions.title: Start Cooking Vegetables
instructions.text: Begin by adding some of the harder vegetables like carrots and napa cabbage to the simmering broth. Let them cook for a few minutes until they are tender-crisp. You can also add some tofu to warm through. The broth will gain more flavor as vegetables cook.

instructions.title: Shabu Shabu the Beef
instructions.text: Each diner takes a slice of beef with their chopsticks and swishes it in the hot broth for just a few seconds until it changes color and is cooked to their liking. The name Shabu Shabu comes from the sound of the meat swishing in the pot. Dip the cooked beef and vegetables into your preferred dipping sauce and enjoy.

instructions.title: Enjoy and Repeat
instructions.text: Continue cooking vegetables and beef in batches, adding more ingredients to the pot as needed. Maintain a gentle simmer in the broth throughout the meal. The beauty of Shabu Shabu is its interactive and relaxed dining style, allowing everyone to enjoy at their own pace.

tips: For the best flavor, use good quality, fresh kombu for your dashi broth. Do not let the kombu boil to prevent a slimy texture and bitter taste.
Ensure your beef is sliced extremely thin. If you cannot find pre-sliced hot pot beef, freeze a block of ribeye or sirloin for 30-60 minutes to make it easier to slice thinly at home.

To finish the meal, after all ingredients are cooked, you can add cooked rice or udon noodles to the flavorful leftover broth (known as zosui) and cook until it absorbs the broth, creating a delicious porridge.
article: Shabu Shabu Thinly Sliced Beef Hot Pot is a beloved Japanese Dinner Recipes dish enjoyed across Japanese. This authentic recipe brings traditional flavors to your kitchen.
